attributes.stem: A 61-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department of his local country hospital with a two-hour history of vomiting blood. He is confused and unable to provide a clear history, however his wife says that he has vomited bright red blood four to five times over this period. He has a medical history of Child-Pugh B cirrhosis and was last seen by a gastroenterologist three years ago. He has refused endoscopies in the past. He drinks around 60 units of alcohol per week and smokes 15 cigarettes daily. On examination, he appears disorientated to time and place. His pulse rate is 105/min and blood pressure 100/72 mmHg. He is noted to have jaundice, splenomegaly and multiple spider naevi.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following medications is most strongly indicated in addition to a proton pump inhibitor and ceftriaxone?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a history of cirrhosis (likely secondary to chronic alcohol misuse) and presents with symptoms and signs suggestive of a massive variceal haemorrhage. He has not seen a gastroenterologist in over three years, so it is unlikely that he has had any recent screening endoscopies. Oesophageal varices arise due to portal hypertension, which leads to the development of porto-systemic collaterals. Rupture of these varices can result in severe and sudden blood loss, often proving fatal.

In this case, the initial priority is to stabilise the patient, especially given his current haemodynamic compromise. This is achieved through the careful administration of crystalloid fluids (and blood products if required), as well as a vasoactive medication such as terlipressin. Terlipressin is a vasopressin analogue which helps to decompress the portal circulation and reduce blood flow through the porto-systemic collaterals. It is the only individual vasoactive agent demonstrated to reduce mortality in variceal bleeding compared to placebo and can be delivered more conveniently as intermittent bolus dosing rather than a continuous infusion, and is therefore generally the preferred agent in practice.

Somatostatin and octreotide are another vasoactive agents which can be used in variceal bleeding to decompress the portal circulation. These are delivered as continuous infusions and may be preferred in patients who are at higher risk of ischaemic complications due to terlipressin's vasoconstrictive side effects, such as those with significant coronary artery or peripheral vascular disease, or when terlipressin is not available.

Vasopressin, another vasoactive agent which was previously used for variceal bleeding, has more side effects compared to terlipressin and is no longer recommended for this indication.

Propranolol and nadolol are non-cardioselective beta-blockers which can used longer-term to help prevent the development of oesophageal varices. As such, they do not have a major role in the acute management of variceal haemorrhage.

Tranexamic acid was previously thought to have had a role as an adjunct in the management of acute variceal bleeding. However, a recent multi-centre international randomised placebo-controlled trial (HALT-IT) found no reduction in mortality from the administration of tranexamic acid in upper gastrointestinal bleeding.

All cirrhotic patients presenting with possible variceal bleeding should be commenced on intravenous antibiotic such as ceftriaxone due to the significant risk of developing a serious infection. A proton pump inhibitor is also often commenced as peptic ulcer disease remains a prominent cause of upper gastrointestinal bleeding in patients with cirrhosis.
learningPoints: Variceal bleeding is a life-threatening complication of cirrhosis that requires prompt management.
Initial management of variceal bleeding includes fluid resuscitation, antibiotics, and vasoactive drugs.
Terlipressin is an effective first-line vasoactive agent in the reduction of portal venous flow.
references: Ioannou, G., Doust, J., & Rockey, D. C. (2003). Terlipressin for acute esophageal variceal hemorrhage. The Cochrane database of systematic reviews, (1), CD002147. https://doi.org/10.1002/14651858.CD002147
Tripathi D, Stanley AJ, Hayes PC, et alUK guidelines on the management of variceal haemorrhage in cirrhotic patientsGut 2015;64:1680-1704.
Edelson JC, Basso JE, Rockey DC. Updated strategies in the management of acute variceal hemorrhage. Curr Opin Gastroenterol. 2021; 37: 167–172.
Gralnek IM, Garcia-Pagan JC, Hernandez-Gea V. Challenges in the Management of Esophagogastric  Varices and Variceal Hemorrhage in Cirrhosis − A  Narrative Review. Am J Med 2024; 137:210−17.
